headline: Kristang Language Down to 2,150 Speakers Worldwide
context: UNESCO's 2025 Atlas of Endangered Languages counts only 2,150 speakers of Kristang — the Malacca-Portuguese creole — making it one of the world's most critically endangered languages.

cards.big: Kristang has been spoken in Malacca for 500 years.
cards.sub: Only 2,150 speakers remain. Without intervention, the language will die within 2 generations.

cards.big: The Codex Kristang project has created the first written grammar.
cards.sub: Community-led revitalisation began in 2016. No government funding has been provided.

cards.big: New Zealand funds Maori language revitalisation at NZD 300M annually.
cards.sub: Malaysia allocates RM0 for any endangered language revitalisation programme.

cards.big: The National Language Act promotes Bahasa Malaysia only.
cards.sub: No legislation supports heritage language preservation. The policy framework recognises one language.

cards.big: A 500-year-old language with zero government support. When it dies, it will be because we chose not to save it.

Language diversity is cultural infrastructure. Malaysia's endangered languages — Kristang, Semai, Mah Meri, dozens of others — deserve a national preservation framework before they become academic footnotes.
